chunks.text: So, this is the final video in the series. I've covered pretty much everything that you need to know to get started inside of Adobe Premiere Pro. But the last step is how do we take this from Premiere and turn this into a video file? How do we export? Well, let me show you. So, all you have to do to export, there's a few different ways. You've got the quick export, which is this option in the top right. So, if you select quick export, you can select a file name and location. You can select a preset. So, for example, match source, adaptive high bit rate, or you can add a custom setting, and you just press export. Alternatively though, I like a little bit more control on the export. So, I'm going to go up to file, export media, and this brings up the export media window. Now, as you can see, this is the video clip that we have created. So, this was our speed ramping effect that we created. We've got our transitions happening here. This is our flash and this is our slide transition. All the fun stuff we've done throughout this project. How do I export this? Well, this is going to be the file name. So, let's call this um test. You've got your location. So, if you select that, you can put that anywhere you want on the drive. You've got presets here, so you can match the source with a high bit rate. Uh you've got all of these other settings here, but let's jump into the settings so we know what we're doing moving forward. Format. Nine times out of 10, I just go for H.264. It's a fairly compressed file format that plays on most devices. I'm pretty sure it's compatible across Windows and Mac. It's pretty compressed, so the file sizes are a bit smaller than a QuickTime file, for example. But of course, if you did want to get more specific, let's say you're exporting for TV, then you may have to go into QuickTime and then Nvidia, you'd have to choose a ProRes 444 or 444XQ codec. chunks.text: Multi-cam editing is a really quick and efficient way of cutting down your videos if you've got multiple takes of the same thing. So, for example, if you have filmed an interview, if you filmed a show, if you filmed a music video, using the multi-cam editor is a really quick and easy way to cut through all of your individual clips and get to a final result much quicker. And I'm going to show you how to do that right now. So, as you can see on my timeline, I have got four clips. Now, these are from a music video. These are different performance shots. And rather than stacking them all on top of each other and picking my favorite bits, I can do an initial first edit by using the multicam editor. So to do that, I'm just going to stack every individual clip onto a different layer. So this is the first clip. So video one, audio one. There we go. They're stacked on top of each other like this. Now video two and audio two can go onto a separate channel. This third clip, this can go three and three. And this clip here can go four and four. Now, from here, we're just going to use the autos synchronization feature to highlight all of these and sync them together. So, highlight everything, right click, synchronize. We're going to go audio track channel. So, the autos synchronization feature is essentially going to listen to the audio on every single track and find points that are in sync and just basically marry those up with each other. If it does fail though, then you can just manually sync these in by listening to certain sync points. But the autosync feature is definitely a game changer.
